text: Turkic Sufism as a cultural and anthropological phenomenon has been described in the terms of the ideals of the mystic perfection, the Absolute and the archetype of the “perfect man”. Despite its considerable influence, this phenomenon has not been studied from the sociological perspective in sufficient detail. Focusing on the motives of Sufism in ancient Turkic nomadic culture, this article applies some sociological theories such as the theory of collective rituals of authority and cultural capital. Based on the Émile Durkheim’s theory of collective effervescence, we can say that the Sufi rituals and practices created a feeling of unity and religious mutual understanding between the Turkic nomadic peoples. Using the Max Weber’s theory of charismatic authority we may say that the Turkic Sufi saints and leaders with their own spiritual charisma promoted new Islamic trends and ideologies and in this way brought a change in the Turkic culture. Based on the Pierre Bourdieu’s theory of cultural capital we can say that the Sufi knowledge, practices and symbolic forms have been an important part of cultural capital for the Turkic nomadic peoples. With this capital they identified themselves as members of the worldwide Islamic civilization and at the same time created their own peculiar system of morals and values. The synthesis of pre-Islamic Tengriism and Sufism in the Great Steppe and its impact on the Turkic nomadic culture is also examined in this paper. This sociological approach can provide the necessary explanation for the considerable influence of Sufism on Turkic nomadic culture and civilization.
